segs.utf8:
Our brain and our skin develop
from the same group of cells

in the early embryo,
and they're intimately linked.

When the brain is under stress,
your skin can feel it, too.

Here's how humans evolved to feel stress
as a natural response to threats.

In small doses, it can help us be
more alert and react appropriately.

Stress can become harmful when we start
to feel like you cannot control it,
or that it is unpredictable.
Stress activates the amygdala,

a part of the brain involved
in primitive emotions.

This signals to a command center
called the hypothalamus,

which triggers glands to make various
hormones like cortisol and adrenaline.

These travel in the bloodstream all
over the body, including to your skin.

We are wired from head
to toe to feel stress.

As well as receiving hormones
from the brain's stress response.

The skin releases
its own stress chemicals too.

The skin will talk to your brain and
your brain will talk to your skin.

And then the effects
of those combined is what causes
the skin changes that we can see.

The stress chemicals
can boost inflammation

and reduce the fatty lipids
that make up the outer skin barrier.

This can make the skin more porous,
leaving it
feeling drier or more sensitive.

What we tend to lose from the skin
barrier is important things like water.

We also can become more sensitive
to things in the outside environment.

So for example,
allergens like pollen

or someone else's fragrance get
into the skin and cause irritation.

A number of small studies looked at
stress and measured the skin barrier

by pulling a piece of sticky tape
off the skin,

then seeing how quickly it recovers
and stops water escaping in one study,

volunteers
were told to deliver a speech,

then solve a maths problem
in front of a hostile audience.

Their skin barrier recovered
more slowly compared to

a non-stressed group who were asked
to simply read an article on their own.

Stress also activates cells
within the skin

to release chemicals like histamine
that make you feel itchy.

Supercharging what's called
the itch scratch cycle.

You feel itchy, you scratch,
you cause further skin damage,

and that triggers you to feel
even more itchy.
So you scratch again.

At the same time,
stress reduces antimicrobial peptides
in the skin.

Small molecules
that normally kill off germs,

making infections like cold sores
or shingles more likely.

What's more, the stress chemicals are
thought to stimulate glands in the skin

to make more of an oily substance called
sebum, which can contribute to spots.

This might be one
of the reasons why

people with more stress also tend to
have worse acne in various studies.

For example, one small study looked
at medical students early

in the academic quarter
and again just before an exam.

The more their stress increased,
the worse their acne got.

Stress can trigger flare ups
in other skin conditions

like eczema, psoriasis,
and hives, too.

And while it's less researched, there is
some evidence that stress might change

and reduce the amount of collagen and
other building blocks of the skin.

Then technically,
you can experience issues like loss

of firmness, loss of suppleness,
increasing lines and wrinkles.
So what can you do about it?

Noticing the stressor
is the first thing.

And and we can notice the stress
on our skin.

You might feel some neck pain
or your stomach in a nod.

Difficulty sleeping.
Forgetting things. Irritability.
Experts say exercise, seeing friends,

and even meditation can help relieve
the stress your brain's feeling.
But what about the skin?

Simple things
that you can do yourself

are to think about your skin barrier
in your normal skincare.

Looking for more hydrating products?
For example,

if your skin is feeling dry,
I normally recommend diets that are good

in healthy fats,
lean proteins, fresh fruit,

and vegetables,
making sure you're well hydrated.

Some experts say it can take three
months to know if any treatments

or lifestyle changes
are really improving the skin,
so consistency is important.

If you have a skin condition
that is aggravated by stress,

it's of course important
to address the stress element

and address
the actual skin condition.

Skin is a manifestation
of the health of your body.
